title: Avoid Overdraft Fees
summary: Take steps to avoid spending more than what is in a bank account.
learnerPromise: You will be able to monitor your balance and use simple habits to avoid overdraft charges.
whyItMatters: Overdraft fees can turn a small purchase into an expensive mistake, and building awareness of your balance helps you stay in control of your money.
realLifeUses: Checking your balance before making a purchase to make sure funds are available
Setting up a low-balance alert so your bank warns you before you go negative
Opting out of overdraft coverage so small purchases are declined instead of causing fees
Linking a savings account as a backup to cover unexpected shortfalls
result: You have turned on at least one guardrail to prevent overdraft fees.
youWillLearn: Check your available balance before spending
Understand how overdraft fees work and when they kick in
Set up account alerts for low balances
Know the difference between account balance and available balance
miniChallenge: Check your current account balance right now and set up a low-balance alert in your banking app.
steps: Log into your banking app and look at your available balance (not just the total balance).
Read your bank's fee schedule to learn what they charge for overdrafts.
Look in your app settings for alert options and turn on low-balance notifications.
Consider opting out of overdraft protection if you prefer purchases to be declined rather than paid with a fee.
Make it a habit to check your balance before any non-essential purchase.
completionCriteria: You can explain the difference between account balance and available balance
You have enabled at least one alert or protection setting in your banking app
commonProblems: Pending transactions that have not cleared yet, making your balance look higher than it is
Automatic subscriptions or bills coming out at unexpected times
Multiple small overdrafts each generating a separate fee on the same day
tips: Some banks offer grace periods or waive the first overdraft fee each year; check your bank's policy
A buffer of even $20–$50 in your account can prevent most accidental overdrafts
If you do get an overdraft fee, calling your bank and asking politely may get it reversed, especially if it is your first time
